findings.summary: Two competing @mcp_tool decorator implementations exist: one in model_context_protocol/decorators.py and a different one in model_context_protocol/discovery/__init__.py. They have different signatures, different metadata keys (schema vs parameters, category vs tags), and different attribute names on the wrapped function. This splits the auto-discovery pipeline across two incompatible paths.
findings.evidence: decorators.py defines mcp_tool with params: name, description, schema, category, version, deprecated_in and sets func._mcp_tool and func._mcp_tool_meta with keys 'schema', 'category'. discovery/__init__.py defines a separate mcp_tool with params: name, description, tags, version, requires and sets func._mcp_tool_meta with keys 'parameters', 'tags'. The discovery engine in discovery/__init__.py reads _mcp_tool_meta and uses key 'parameters', but the decorators.py version stores under key 'schema'. The 36 mcp_tools.py files that use a try/except ImportError fallback re-implement yet a third minimal stub.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate to a single canonical @mcp_tool decorator. The two implementations should be merged into one with a clear location. Remove the 36 fallback stub re-implementations from individual mcp_tools.py files.

findings.summary: subprocess.run is called approximately 409 times across 104 files, with no timeout parameter in the core git_operations commands module. This creates unbounded blocking in production paths that service agent calls.
findings.evidence: branching.py lines 18, 46, 79, 103, 135 call subprocess.run with no timeout=. commit.py lines 48+ have no timeout=. model_context_protocol/tools.py lines 320, 326, 342, 385, 425 have no timeout=. The language guidance explicitly flags subprocess calls without timeout as a concern.
findings.suggestion: Add a configurable timeout (e.g. from os.getenv, default 30s) to all subprocess.run calls in production paths.

findings.summary: The data_visualization module contains two parallel hierarchies for the same chart types: charts/ uses standalone create_* functions + class wrappers with matplotlib directly, while plots/ uses a BasePlot dataclass inheritance hierarchy. Both define BarChart, Heatmap, Histogram, LinePlot, ScatterPlot, PieChart, and BoxPlot. The module __init__.py imports from charts/ for most but imports BasePlot for the plots hierarchy.
findings.evidence: charts/bar_chart.py: BarChart class wraps create_bar_chart() function, accepts categories/values, calls matplotlib directly. plots/bar_chart.py: BarChart extends BasePlot dataclass, calls ax.bar(). Both are named BarChart. charts/heatmap.py: create_heatmap() uses numpy + seaborn. plots/heatmap.py: Heatmap extends BasePlot, calls ax.imshow(). The __init__.py imports charts.BarChart (line 25) and charts.Heatmap (line 28) but plots/__init__.py exports its own BarChart and Heatmap. They also coexist with a third engine (engines/advanced_plotter.py with AdvancedPlotter).
findings.suggestion: Choose one chart implementation strategy. Retire either charts/ or plots/. The BasePlot inheritance pattern in plots/ is cleaner and more testable. The charts/ directory with standalone functions has more features. Pick one, deprecate the other.

findings.summary: MCP tool functions use inconsistent return-value error strategies. Some return {'status': 'error', 'error': str(exc)}, others return {'status': 'error', 'message': str(e)}, and some return {'error': '...', 'success': False}. The key for the error message alternates between 'error', 'message', and 'detail' with no consistent schema.
findings.evidence: email/mcp_tools.py line 98: return {'status': 'error', 'error': str(exc)}. cloud/mcp_tools.py line 31: return {'status': 'error', 'message': f'Failed to list instances: {e}'}. agents/mcp_tools.py line 24: return {'status': 'error', 'message': f'Agent not found.'}. model_context_protocol/tools.py line 43: return {'error': ..., 'success': False}. data_visualization/mcp_tools.py line 66: return {'status': 'error', 'message': str(e)}. Callers cannot reliably check for errors without knowing which key schema each tool uses.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a single error envelope: {'status': 'error', 'error': str(exc)} across all mcp_tools.py files. Extract a shared helper _mcp_error(exc) that produces this envelope.

findings.summary: scrape/core.py has its module docstring placed after the import block rather than before it, and the docstring text contains what appears to be a commented-out logger line that was accidentally included in the docstring body. This suggests copy-paste of a partially formed file.
findings.evidence: Lines 1-12 of scrape/core.py: imports appear at lines 1-4, then a string literal at line 6 starting with triple-quote that contains 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' at line 9. This makes the string literal the module docstring but it contains embedded dead code-looking text: "logger = get_logger(__name__)\nThis module defines...". The logger import is missing from this file even though the docstring mentions it.
findings.suggestion: Fix the module docstring placement (must be the first statement) and remove the stray logger line from the docstring text.

findings.summary: 36 mcp_tools.py files each individually re-implement the same try/except ImportError fallback stub for the @mcp_tool decorator. This is pure boilerplate with no policy or added logic, repeated verbatim.
findings.evidence: Each file has identical block: try: from codomyrmex.model_context_protocol.decorators import mcp_tool / except ImportError: / def mcp_tool(**kwargs): / def decorator(func): / func._mcp_tool_meta = kwargs / return func / return decorator. Some variants use **kwargs: Any and # type: ignore[misc], others omit the type annotation. Found in 36 files (confirmed by grep count).
findings.suggestion: model_context_protocol.decorators should never be missing at runtime (it is a core module with no optional deps). Remove the try/except fallback entirely, or if a fallback is genuinely needed for bootstrap isolation, put it in a single shared location.

findings.summary: The @mcp_tool decorator in model_context_protocol/decorators.py sets both func._mcp_tool and func._mcp_tool_meta to the same dict object. This is redundant — the dual attribute assignment serves no discernible purpose since the discovery engine only reads _mcp_tool_meta.
findings.evidence: decorators.py lines 70-71: func._mcp_tool = tool_meta / func._mcp_tool_meta = tool_meta. Lines 86-87: wrapper._mcp_tool = tool_meta / wrapper._mcp_tool_meta = tool_meta. Both attributes point to the same object. The discovery engine (discovery/__init__.py line 251) only checks hasattr(obj, '_mcp_tool_meta').
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant _mcp_tool attribute; keep only _mcp_tool_meta. If _mcp_tool is used elsewhere, document why.

findings.summary: sentence-transformers, chromadb, docker, pylint, bandit, radon, lizard, seaborn, scipy, numpy, redis, paramiko, and google-generativeai are all listed as non-optional core dependencies. Several of these (chromadb, sentence-transformers, docker) are heavy SDKs that pull in large transitive dependency trees but are only used by specific modules.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml lines 35-108: sentence-transformers>=2.0.0, chromadb>=0.4.0, docker>=6.0.0, pylint>=3.0.0, bandit>=1.8.0, radon>=6.0.0, lizard>=1.17.0 all listed under [project.dependencies] (non-optional). redis>=5.0.0, paramiko>=3.3.0, aiohttp>=3.13.3, msgpack>=1.1.2, fastavro>=1.12.1 also non-optional. The architecture claims lazy module loading but the install cost is paid unconditionally.
findings.suggestion: Move SDKs used only by specific modules (chromadb, sentence-transformers, docker, redis, paramiko) to [project.optional-dependencies] matching their module. Core should only require what the CLI entry point needs at startup.

findings.summary: The PAI MCP bridge critical path — discovery.py's _discover_dynamic_tools, server.py's get_tool_registry, and the TTL cache — has no dedicated unit tests found. The bridge is the primary integration point (198 tools) but the test directory for it is empty __init__.py files only.
findings.evidence: Glob of src/codomyrmex/tests/unit/agents/ shows only __init__.py stubs plus higher-level integration tests (test_integrations.py, test_orchestration.py). No test file for pai/mcp/discovery.py, pai/mcp/server.py, or pai/trust_gateway.py is visible in the unit test tree. The discovery cache logic (TTL, locking, invalidation) and registry assembly are untested directly.
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests for _discover_dynamic_tools (cache hit, cache miss, TTL expiry), get_tool_registry (static + dynamic merge), and trust_gateway state transitions.

findings.summary: Two files (engines/plotter.py and engines/advanced_plotter.py) contain identical copy-pasted fallback no-op implementations for monitor_performance decorator and performance_context context manager. The no-op classes include docstrings that redundantly restate the obvious ('No-op decorator', 'Intentional no-op').
findings.evidence: plotter.py lines 44-59: def monitor_performance(*args, **kwargs): / def decorator(func): / return func / return decorator and class performance_context with __enter__/__exit__ returning None with 'Intentional no-op' comment. advanced_plotter.py lines 29-45: identical block. The comment 'Intentional no-op' on a return None statement inside a context manager __exit__ is a classic AI-generated restatement comment.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single _noop_performance module or inline guard. Do not duplicate the fallback across files.

findings.summary: Multiple short wrapper functions and dunder methods have multi-line docstrings that restate the function name or signature verbatim, adding no information. Examples include __str__ docstring 'Return human-readable string', __repr__ docstring 'Return string representation', and wrapper() docstring 'Wrapper.'.
findings.evidence: plots/_base.py line 83: def __str__(self): '"""Return human-readable string."""' — this is the Python default for __str__ and says nothing. decorators.py line 45: def decorator(func): '"""Decorator."""' — one-word docstring on a 40-line function. decorators.py line 74: def wrapper(*args, **kwargs): '"""Wrapper."""' — restatement of name. decorators.py line 46: '"""Decorator."""' on the outer decorator. These are AI-generated placeholder docstrings that survived.
findings.suggestion: Remove trivial docstrings that restate the function name. For decorator() and wrapper() at the decorator pattern level, omit the docstring entirely or describe what the decorator does specifically.

findings.summary: data_visualization contains three separate visualization engine paths: charts/ (standalone create_* functions + class wrappers), plots/ (BasePlot inheritance hierarchy with 20 subclasses), and engines/ (AdvancedPlotter class + plotter.py facade). These serve the same purpose without documented role boundaries. The module __init__.py imports from charts/ for some types, plots/ for MermaidDiagram only, and does not expose engines/ at all.
findings.evidence: __init__.py imports: BarChart from charts.bar_chart (line 25), Histogram from charts.histogram (line 29), but also imports BasePlot-based MermaidDiagram from plots.mermaid (line 38). The plots/ directory has 20+ chart implementations (bar_chart, histogram, line_plot, heatmap, scatter, box, pie, area, violin, radar, candlestick, gantt, funnel, sankey, wordcloud, confusion_matrix, treemap, network). The charts/ directory has 8 of the same types. Neither __init__.py nor any README documents which is canonical.
findings.suggestion: Designate one primary implementation path for each chart type. Retire the redundant hierarchy. The plots/ BasePlot pattern is more testable; charts/ has more production use. Document the role of engines/ as the advanced/statistical path.

findings.summary: The data_visualization module presents three incompatible public interfaces at its boundary: the charts/ functional API (create_bar_chart taking categories+values), the plots/ OOP API (BasePlot subclasses taking data+options), and the mcp_tools.py API (generate_chart taking chart_type+dict with special-cased bar vs others). A caller cannot predict which abstraction level or which BarChart will be returned.
findings.evidence: mcp_tools.py lines 44-48: if chart_type == 'bar': result = factory(data, title=title) else: result = factory(**data). This special-cases bar charts because the charts/bar_chart.py create_bar_chart has a different signature from the others (takes a data dict, others take **kwargs). The __init__.py create_bar_chart (line 48) wraps BarChart from charts/ but the generate_chart MCP tool imports from codomyrmex.data_visualization and calls dv.create_bar_chart differently from other types.
findings.suggestion: Define a single public interface contract for all chart types (e.g. ChartSpec dataclass) and route all callers through it. Consolidate the three access paths into one.

findings.summary: discovery.py uses two different timestamp variables for cache TTL management: _CACHE_EXPIRY (a monotonic float for the cache expiry time) and a separately referenced _CACHE_TIMESTAMP = 0.0 mentioned in _tool_invalidate_cache but not defined at module level. The invalidate function sets global _CACHE_TIMESTAMP = 0.0 but the actual cache check reads _CACHE_EXPIRY. This is an inconsistency that could leave the cache in an unexpected state.
findings.evidence: discovery.py line 29: global _CACHE_TIMESTAMP / _CACHE_TIMESTAMP = 0.0 inside _tool_invalidate_cache — but _CACHE_TIMESTAMP is never declared at module level. Line 46: _CACHE_EXPIRY: float | None = None is the actual cache expiry variable. Line 116: the cache check reads _CACHE_EXPIRY. Lines 150-151: the cache set writes _CACHE_EXPIRY. The invalidate path at line 52 (invalidate_tool_cache) correctly sets _CACHE_EXPIRY = None but _tool_invalidate_cache at line 30 sets an undefined _CACHE_TIMESTAMP = 0.0 instead of calling invalidate_tool_cache directly.
findings.suggestion: Remove the _CACHE_TIMESTAMP reference from _tool_invalidate_cache and use the existing invalidate_tool_cache() function. Declare _CACHE_TIMESTAMP at module level or eliminate it.

findings.summary: The generate_chart MCP tool dispatches to chart factories via a string-keyed dict and then branches on chart_type == 'bar' to decide whether to unpack data as positional args or keyword args. This couples the dispatch logic to knowledge of individual factory signatures, making it fragile and non-uniform.
findings.evidence: mcp_tools.py lines 28-48: chart_factories dict with 6 entries. Lines 44-48: if chart_type == 'bar': result = factory(data, title=title) else: result = factory(**data). The comment says 'Some factories might have different signatures, mapping basic ones.' The bar branch passes (data, title=title) while all others do factory(**data). If any other factory has a non-**kwargs signature this will silently fail or produce wrong output.
findings.suggestion: Standardize all chart factory signatures to accept a single ChartSpec or uniform dict. Remove the per-type branching in the dispatcher.

findings.summary: AdvancedPlotter._get_color_palette is a 22-branch if/elif chain that maps each ColorPalette enum member to its string name for sns.color_palette. Since each branch body is structurally identical except for the string literal, this is a structural pattern that should be a data mapping.
findings.evidence: advanced_plotter.py lines 178-199: 10 elif branches, each containing only return sns.color_palette('<palette_name>', n_colors). The pattern is: each ColorPalette enum value's .value attribute already holds the string name (VIRIDIS = 'viridis', PLASMA = 'plasma', etc.), so the entire method could be replaced with return sns.color_palette(self.config.palette.value if self.config.palette != ColorPalette.DEFAULT else 'husl', n_colors). The same pattern appears in _setup_style (6 elif branches for style mapping).
findings.suggestion: Replace both switch chains with direct use of enum .value attributes, which already carry the seaborn string. The DEFAULT case needs a fallback string. One line each.
